policy_statement: Regions and Autonomous Provinces commit to orient their policies as far as possible toward reducing greenhouse gases and to coordinate central-state and local initiatives/financing with sustainability as a priority objective.
mechanism_description: The regions undertake to orient policies toward reducing greenhouse gases and to coordinate central-state and local initiatives and financing with sustainability as a priority objective, supporting an overall emissions-reduction pathway.
primary_causal_pathway: Policy/finance governance alignment → accelerated low-carbon investments & coordinated implementation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Public-sector governance—setting cross-level policy direction, coordination mechanisms, and sustainable-finance rules/criteria that guide how programs are designed and how capital is allocated.  
(2) Mediating process: Improved alignment reduces policy fragmentation and investment risk, increases deployment of low-carbon technologies and practices, and shifts public/private capital away from high-emitting activities toward mitigation measures.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced territorial and/or consumption-based greenhouse gas emissions over time (often economy-wide), contributing to climate change mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is economy-wide ↓ GHG emissions by steering and coordinating policies and finance toward decarbonization actions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Governance and finance alignment is used to enable/scale decarbonization actions, whose primary physical outcome is ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: The mechanism’s defining intent and typical largest effect is emissions reduction; any resource-efficiency gains or adaptation benefits are contingent on which projects are financed, not inherent to the mechanism itself.  
3) Edge cases applied: If “sustainability” were implemented mainly as resilience spending or ecological restoration without explicit emissions focus, classification could shift; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly targets a GHG-reduction pathway and emissions-oriented alignment.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy fitting this mechanism (policy coordination + sustainable-finance alignment explicitly oriented to GHG reduction) primarily operates by enabling emissions-reducing actions across sectors, so Mitigation remains the correct primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., energy transition finance, transport decarbonization coordination, industrial decarbonization) require policy-level details about funded/mandated measures and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential ambiguity if “sustainability” is interpreted broadly and implemented mainly through non-mitigation objectives (e.g., adaptation-only investments); ensure the mechanism instance is explicitly oriented to GHG reduction or decarbonization-aligned finance to keep Mitigation as primary.
key_indicators: “policies shall be oriented… towards the reduction of greenhouse gases”; “initiatives and financing… shall be coordinated”; “priority objective of sustainability”
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health improvements; broader environmental sustainability benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: “Commit/undertake to guarantee” could resemble a target-setting pledge, but no explicit quantified target or timeline is stated; the operative mechanism is cross-level policy/finance coordination, best captured as a planning/strategy instrument.
